summary:
The user, Analyst 3, began their shift to definitively prove a TLS certificate issue was the root cause of a major service outage. They immediately investigated the Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS) audit logs, a critical gap from the previous shift. The logs revealed a "partial_success" status where a nightly job deployed 119 of 312 certificates (38.1%) with an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN) due to a permissive "soft-fail" configuration. The user declared this the root cause, noting the 38.1% figure perfectly matched the pod failure rate.

To gain irrefutable proof before escalating, the user cross-referenced a specific certificate serial number from a prior error log with the faulty certificate data, confirming a perfect match. With this evidence, they reconstructed the full causal chain, proving the previously suspected database pool exhaustion was a downstream symptom. The user escalated the incident to SEV-0, monitored the successful canary deployment of an emergency fix, and created two new red flags to investigate the underlying code bug and the "soft-fail" setting that allowed the incident to occur.
key_facts: The user analyzed CLS audit logs for job `cls-rotate-20260315-0100`, which had a "partial_success" status.
The job deployed 119 out of 312 certificates (38.1%) with an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN): `*.mesh.internal` instead of `*.mesh.local`.
Deployment of the bad certificates occurred because the system was in a "soft-fail mode".
The 38.1% certificate failure rate precisely matched the pod failure rate identified in a previous shift.
The user confirmed the root cause by matching a certificate serial number (`7A:3B:91:EE:04:C1`) from a pod error log to a certificate with the incorrect SAN.
The full causal chain was established: a CLS job deployed bad certs, causing mTLS failures, which led to hung application threads and subsequent DB connection pool exhaustion.
Based on the evidence, the user escalated the incident to SEV-0.
The canary deployment of the fix (CHG-89104) was successful, with remediated pods showing immediate recovery.
Two new red flags were created: C-RF1 to investigate the root code change (`PR #4781`) and C-RF2 to investigate the use of 'soft-fail mode'.
positions_taken: The CLS audit logs were the most critical piece of missing evidence to investigate.
A "partial_success" status with an exit code of 0 is a major red flag.
Irrefutable proof was required before escalating the incident severity.
The SAN mismatch in the certificates was definitively the root cause of the entire incident.
The 'soft-fail' configuration was a key contributing factor that allowed the incident to happen.
positions_changed: The user's position on the root cause solidified from a well-supported hypothesis (based on handover) to an irrefutably proven fact after cross-referencing certificate data.
This session definitively confirmed the shift away from the initial DB pool leak theory by establishing it as a downstream symptom in a complete causal chain.
